Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have these bursts where I'd study for hours, downloading everything I needed, and then I wouldn't touch the course for days. I'm enrolled in so many online courses that I kind of bounce between them without a real schedule. When I did sit down to study, I preferred visual materials like diagrams and videos that I could screenshot and review later on my own time.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how scared I was about failing. Some weeks I'd barely look at anything, then panic would hit and I'd cram intensely. Deadlines were my main motivator, not any kind of regular schedule. I know it's not the healthiest approach, but with 17 courses, I couldn't keep a consistent routine for each one.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd suddenly remember an assignment or get anxious about missing something important. Sometimes the gamified tasks would send notifications that made me curious enough to check in. Other times I'd log in just to download materials so I could study them offline later when I felt more focused.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. The closest it got was when the gamified elements made it feel less like a chore and more like something I could actually engage with. But even then, I'd go through phases of logging in regularly and then completely disappearing for a while.
Q5: The video content was most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could actually see concepts being explained. I also liked the gamified tasks because they broke things down into smaller, less overwhelming pieces. When something had points or badges attached, it felt more achievable and less scary than staring at a massive reading list.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the discussion forums and lengthy reading materials. The readings felt endless and I'd get anxious just looking at them, so I'd skip to videos or diagrams instead. Discussion posts felt performative to me, like I was just posting to get credit rather than actually learning anything meaningful from them.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand something for the exam or if it had some kind of reward attached. The gamified elements really helped with this because I could see my progress. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from what I'd actually be tested on, I'd probably skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford with my course load. Required ones created anxiety because I knew I had to do them, but at least that pressure got me moving. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that looks interesting' but then never actually do it because something required was always more urgent.
Q9: Absolutely, this was basically my main study method. I'd download PDFs, screenshot slides, and save videos to watch offline. I have notebooks full of hand-drawn diagrams and visual notes that I made from course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my laptop, just me and my notes.
Q10: Most of my exam prep happened offline. I'd print materials or work from screenshots on my tablet, drawing concept maps and color-coding information. I'd also study with friends sometimes, quizzing each other verbally. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but it's how I actually learned the material well enough to score high on exams.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online, maybe twice, then I'd have my notes and wouldn't need to go back to the LMS. I'd access a PDF, download it immediately, and never open it through the platform again. My offline study time far exceeded anything the LMS could track.
Q12: There were definitely weeks where everything felt overwhelming and I just couldn't engage. When I was juggling multiple deadlines across different courses, this one would sometimes get pushed aside. The fear and anxiety would build up until it became paralyzing rather than motivating. During those times, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because logging in just reminded me how behind I was.
Q13: Fatigue hit me hard, especially managing so many courses at once. When I was exhausted, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Confusion also affected me because if I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes just close the tab rather than push through. My stress levels definitely impacted how productively I used the platform.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd have the LMS open while scrolling my phone or just zoning out completely. I'd click through videos without really watching them or open readings and skim without comprehension. My mind would be elsewhere, worried about other courses or just mentally exhausted.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even though I knew they mattered. The fear of doing them badly made me avoid starting them at all. It's like this weird cycle where I'm scared of failing, so I don't start, which makes me more likely to fail, which makes me more scared. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow until tomorrow became the deadline.
Q16: Deadlines were everything for me. They were basically the only reason I submitted anything. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation I'd been lacking for weeks. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates and flatlined in between. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though I was sometimes scared to look at it. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with similar activities. Negative feedback could either push me to try harder or make me want to avoid that type of task entirely. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal and more like a game score.
Q18: The course structure was honestly a bit overwhelming with so much content. The gamified elements helped break things into manageable chunks, which I appreciated. But the sheer volume of material across my 17 courses meant I had to be selective about what I actually engaged with deeply versus what I just skimmed.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt achievable and gave immediate feedback. What made it harder was when content was only available in long text formats without visual alternatives. Also, having so many different modules and resources made it hard to know what to prioritize, which increased my anxiety about missing something important.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, honestly. You might be logging in, browsing, downloading things, trying to figure out what to do, but then getting overwhelmed and not actually submitting work. Or you're engaging with the content in ways that don't result in submitted assignments, like just trying to understand the material without completing the formal tasks.
Q21: That's literally me. My exam score was really high even though my attendance and assignment completion were low. I studied effectively offline using visual methods that worked for my learning style. The LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams preparing for exams.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you were actually mentally present or learning. They miss all offline study, all the thinking you do away from the screen, and the quality of engagement versus just clicking through. They also can't capture fear or motivation or why someone might be avoiding the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm genuinely curious and thinking critically about concepts, not just trying to check boxes. For me, real engagement often happens offline when I'm drawing diagrams or explaining concepts to myself. It's about mental investment, not just time spent logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I often felt, or how much anxiety affected my participation patterns. It wouldn't capture my extensive offline studying or the visual learning strategies that actually helped me succeed. It also couldn't show that despite my low attendance and assignment completion, I was genuinely learning and understanding the material in my own way.
